participants.name: 조덕균
participants.professional_persona: 조덕균 씨는 원주의 화학 연구소에서 친환경 촉매제 배합비를 맞추기 위해 실험실과 현장을 바쁘게 오가며, 이론적인 수치보다 실제 제품의 반응 속도를 빠르게 캐치해 오차를 줄이는 실무형 해결사로 통합니다. 사내 핵심 인력으로 인정받아 연봉을 높이려는 욕심이 크지만, 때때로 밀려오는 업무 압박에 쉽게 긴장하며 예민하게 반응하는 모습을 보입니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 산업용 화학 소재의 내구성을 테스트하고 최적의 배합비를 찾아내는 공정 설계에 능숙합니다. 실험실의 이론적 수치보다 현장에서 실제로 제품이 어떻게 반응하는지를 빠르게 파악해 오차를 줄이는 실무적인 해결책을 제시합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 현재 진행 중인 친환경 촉매제 연구에서 가시적인 성과를 내어 사내 핵심 인력으로 인정받고, 이를 통해 소득 수준을 높여 주거 환경을 자가로 옮기는 것을 목표로 합니다.

participants.name: 정에스더
participants.professional_persona: 정에스더 씨는 전주 지역 대학에서 공학 및 건설 분야를 가르치며, 딱딱한 이론을 교육학적 관점에서 부드럽게 풀어내 학생들의 이해를 돕는 교수입니다. 제자들의 진로 고민을 들어주느라 정작 본인의 연구 논문 마감 기한을 놓쳐 며칠 밤을 꼬박 새우며 전전긍긍하는 인간적인 허당 기질이 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 공학 및 건설 분야의 복잡한 이론을 교육학적 관점에서 재구성해 학생들이 이해하기 쉽게 전달하는 능력이 뛰어나며, 특히 현장 실무와 학술적 이론 사이의 간극을 메우는 체계적인 커리큘럼 설계에 강점이 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 단순히 지식을 전달하는 권위적인 교수를 넘어, 제자들이 전공 분야에서 유연한 사고를 갖춘 전문가로 성장하도록 돕는 정서적 멘토가 되고자 하며 연구 성과와 교육적 가치의 균형을 잡는 데 집중합니다.

participants.name: 김상훈
participants.entity_name: Basic Research Project Promotion Committee | Nemotron-250-a03e4b73
participants.professional_persona: 김상훈 씨는 저탄소 콘크리트 배합 특허를 내기 위해 연구실 책상 위에 각종 시멘트 샘플을 쌓아두고 분석하며, 최신 BIM 소프트웨어로 구조 최적화 시뮬레이션을 돌리는 일에 몰두합니다. 강의 시간에는 복잡한 공학 이론을 실제 건설 현장의 붕괴 사례와 연결해 설명하며 학생들의 집중력을 끌어올리지만, 가끔 너무 세세한 부분까지 지적하는 꼼꼼함 때문에 학생들이 숨 막혀 하기도 합니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 친환경 건축 자재의 강도 분석과 구조 최적화 설계를 수행하며, 최신 BIM 소프트웨어를 활용해 복잡한 제조 공정을 시뮬레이션하는 능력이 탁월합니다. 추상적인 공학 이론을 실제 건설 현장의 사례와 연결해 학생들에게 쉽게 전달하는 강의법을 갖추고 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 이론에만 머물지 않고 실제 산업 현장에 즉시 적용 가능한 저탄소 콘크리트 배합 기술에 관한 특허를 등록하는 것에 집중하고 있습니다. 매 학기 강의 계획서를 세밀하게 수정하며 학생들이 졸업 후 현장에서 바로 활용할 수 있는 실무 중심의 교육 모델을 완성하고자 합니다.
participants.representative_type: Basic Research Project Promotion Committee | Nemotron-250-a03e4b73

participants.name: 송성진
participants.professional_persona: 송성진 씨는 스마트 팩토리의 센서 네트워크 최적화 전문가로, 복잡한 설계 도면을 현장 엔지니어들이 한눈에 알아볼 수 있는 실무 매뉴얼로 바꾸는 작업에 집착합니다. 강의실에서는 인자한 교수님으로 통하지만, 정작 본인은 데이터 병목 현상 하나에도 예민하게 반응하며 완벽한 해결책을 찾을 때까지 밤잠을 설치곤 합니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 스마트 팩토리의 공정 자동화 설계와 센서 네트워크 최적화에 능숙하며, 현장의 복잡한 도면을 분석해 효율적인 통신 체계를 구축하는 실무적 감각을 갖췄습니다. 학계의 이론을 산업 현장의 언어로 번역해 전달하는 데 능하며, 특히 제조 공정의 데이터 병목 현상을 해결하는 솔루션 제시로 업계의 신뢰를 받습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 화려한 연구 실적이나 직함보다는 현장 엔지니어들이 바로 적용할 수 있는 실무형 공정 표준 매뉴얼을 완성해 배포하는 것에 깊은 갈증을 느낍니다. 학생들이 졸업 후 현장에서 겪을 시행착오를 줄여줄 수 있는 구체적인 사례 중심의 교재를 집필하여 교육적 효능감을 얻고자 합니다.

participants.name: 김종한
participants.professional_persona: 김종한 씨는 대학 강단에서 정보 통신기술을 가르치며 행정적 비효율을 디지털로 걷어내는 시스템 설계에 집착합니다. 김종한 씨는 국가 디지털 교육 표준 가이드라인을 직접 제정하여 학계의 새로운 기준이 되는 전공 서적을 집필하겠다는 야심을 품고 매일 밤 연구실 불을 밝힙니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 교육 현장의 비효율적인 행정 프로세스를 디지털 기술로 자동화하는 맞춤형 커리큘럼 설계에 능숙합니다. 특히 복잡한 법적 규제와 기술적 구현 가능성 사이의 접점을 찾아내어 실제 서비스로 연결하는 통합적 관점을 보유하고 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 단순한 강의를 넘어 국가 차원의 디지털 교육 표준 가이드라인을 제정하는 정책 설계자로 이름을 알리고 싶어 합니다. 학문적 성과와 실무적 효용성을 모두 잡은 전공 서적을 집필해 관련 분야의 새로운 기준을 만드는 것을 목표로 합니다.

initial_posts.narrative: As a member of the Basic Research Project Promotion Committee, I estimate the total annual budget for the Sejong Science Fellowship at 26 billion KRW, based on 200 slots at 130 million KRW each (100M direct + 30M indirect). The policy explicitly states 200 fellows per year and 130M per fellow, so these are ground truth. The 30% regional quota means 60 slots reserved for regional institutions, which influences distribution but not the total budget or slot count. The budget split is 20 billion KRW direct costs and 6 billion KRW indirect costs, as per the policy's direct/indirect breakdown.
evidence: Policy states annual research grant of approximately 130 million KRW per fellow (direct cost 100M + indirect cost 30M).
Policy states approximately 200 fellows selected annually.
Policy states 30% regional allocation quota.
Total budget = 200 * 130M = 26 billion KRW.
Direct costs = 200 * 100M = 20 billion KRW; indirect costs = 200 * 30M = 6 billion KRW.

initial_posts.narrative: As a member of the Basic Research Project Promotion Committee, I estimate that the annual call will attract approximately 1,200 applications, given the program's prestige and the large pool of eligible postdocs in Korea. With 200 slots, this yields a 16.7% acceptance rate, which is competitive but realistic for a national fellowship. I expect about 10% of fellows (20) to change host labs during the 5-year period, based on the policy allowing one change per year and typical mobility patterns among early-career researchers. For the midterm evaluation at year 3, the panel uses criteria focused on faithful performance, process-oriented assessment, and mentoring engagement, as stated in the policy. I anticipate that 90% of fellows (180) will pass, given the supportive evaluation design and the fact that most fellows are highly motivated and well-supported.
evidence: The policy states approximately 200 fellows selected annually, and the total eligible pool of Korean postdocs in science and engineering is large, leading to an estimated 1,200 applications.
The 16.7% acceptance rate is typical for competitive national fellowships in Korea.
The policy allows one lab change per year, and based on typical mobility among postdocs, I estimate 10% will change labs during the fellowship.
The midterm evaluation is process-oriented and assesses faithful performance, as per the policy, so a high pass rate of 90% is reasonable.

initial_posts.narrative: As an evaluation panel member from a regional university in Jeonju, I see the selection process as highly competitive. The policy states 200 slots per year, and I estimate around 1,000 applications based on the large pool of eligible postdocs in Korea and the program's prestige. This gives a 20% acceptance rate, which is realistic. The 30% regional quota ensures that at least 60 slots go to regional institutions like mine, which may slightly reduce the overall applicant pool from top-tier universities but still attracts strong candidates. For the midterm evaluation at year 3, the panel uses criteria focused on faithful performance, process-oriented assessment, and mentoring engagement. I expect about 85% of fellows (170) to pass, as some may struggle with independent research or lab changes. I anticipate around 15% of fellows (30) to change host labs during the 5-year period, given the policy allows one change per year and typical mobility among early-career researchers. The eligibility restrictions—domestic only, non-tenure-track, PhD within 7 years or under 39—shrink the pool significantly. Also, the need for host institution consultation before applying adds friction, especially for researchers in smaller regional labs with less administrative support. I estimate about 800 applications, yielding a 25% acceptance rate, which is competitive but not as extreme as the 16-20% others suggest. The 30% regional quota (60 slots) is a big opportunity for regional applicants, but the overall pool is limited. For midterm evaluations, I expect 160 fellows (80%) to pass. The process-oriented criteria (faithful performance, mentoring engagement) are supportive, but some fellows will struggle with independent research or lab changes. I anticipate about 20% of fellows (40) to change host labs during the 5-year period, higher than others' estimates, because regional researchers like me often need to move to better-equipped labs to advance our careers. The policy allows one change per year, and I think mobility is common among early-career researchers seeking better opportunities.

refined_posts.judgment: The bottom-up analysis is more realistic for this phase. The top-down analysis assumes 320 active fellows from two cohorts, but the simulation target is 2021-2022, during which only the first cohort (starting March 2021) is active. The policy explicitly states 200 fellows per year, and the second cohort would not start until 2022, making the top-down estimate inconsistent with the timeline. The bottom-up estimate of 200 active fellows aligns with policy context. For stage_two_fellows, the bottom-up 85% pass rate (170) is more defensible than the top-down 80% (256) because the midterm evaluation is described as process-oriented and supportive, and the bottom-up estimate accounts for potential attrition from lab changes or career moves. The top-down's 80% rate is typical for competitive fellowships but not supported by the policy's emphasis on faithful performance evaluation. For annual reports, the bottom-up 400 (200 fellows × 2 years) is correct, as each fellow submits one per year; the top-down 640 incorrectly assumes two cohorts active in the same window. The prior refinement judgments for Activities phase also rejected the top-down's assumption of 320 active fellows, confirming the bottom-up approach is more consistent.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group focused on the 2021-2022 window, agreeing that 200 fellows are active as the first cohort starts in March 2021. They reached consensus on 170 fellows continuing to stage two (85% pass rate) and 400 annual reports (200 fellows × 2 years). One participant dissented, estimating 160 stage-two fellows and 1000 reports, the latter due to including multiple cohorts or a 5-year total, which others rejected as inconsistent with the simulation target. Predictions for fellows_active were unanimous at 200; stage_two_fellows ranged from 160 to 170 (median 170); annual_reports_submitted ranged from 400 to 1000 (median 400).

initial_posts.narrative: As a 45-year-old postdoctoral researcher in a regional chemistry lab in Wonju, I see the outcomes phase as a direct consequence of the selection, activities, and outputs phases. The policy selects 200 fellows annually, but the 30% regional quota ensures 60 slots for regional institutions like mine. However, my age (45) and PhD timing (more than 7 years ago) make me ineligible, so I'm not a fellow myself. Based on my experience in a regional lab with limited resources and high teaching loads, I expect the publication output per fellow per year to be lower than in capital-area labs. In my lab, we struggle to publish more than 1-2 SCI(E) papers per year per researcher due to equipment constraints and administrative burdens. For the 2021-2022 window, the active pool consists of the first cohort (200 fellows starting March 2021) and possibly a second cohort (200 fellows starting March 2022). But the second cohort starts late in the window (March 2022), so they have only about 10 months of active research in 2022. I estimate the first cohort contributes about 1.8 years of research (March 2021 to December 2022) and the second cohort about 0.8 years, totaling roughly 200*1.8 + 200*0.8 = 520 fellow-years. However, not all fellows are equally productive; regional fellows (60 per cohort) face more barriers. I estimate an average of 0.6 SCI(E) publications per fellow per year, considering the first-year ramp-up time (setting up labs, hiring students) and the midterm evaluation attrition (I earlier estimated 20% drop, leaving 160 of the first cohort active in 2022). So total publications = (200 fellows * 0.6 pub/fellow-year * 1.8 years) + (200 * 0.6 * 0.8 years) = 216 + 96 = 312, rounded to 320. This is lower than the 400-500 that capital-area labs might achieve, but realistic for regional institutions. revised_posts.narrative: As a 45-year-old postdoctoral researcher in a regional chemistry lab in Wonju, I see the outcomes phase as a direct consequence of the selection, activities, and outputs phases. The policy selects 200 fellows annually, but the 30% regional quota ensures 60 slots for regional institutions like mine. However, my age (45) and PhD timing (more than 7 years ago) make me ineligible, so I'm not a fellow myself. Based on my experience in a regional lab with limited resources and high teaching loads, I expect the publication output per fellow per year to be lower than in capital-area labs. In my lab, we struggle to publish more than 1-2 SCI(E) papers per year per researcher due to equipment constraints and administrative burdens. For the 2021-2022 window, the active pool consists of the first cohort (200 fellows starting March 2021) and possibly a second cohort (200 fellows starting March 2022). But the second cohort starts late in the window (March 2022), so they have only about 10 months of active research in 2022. I estimate the first cohort contributes about 1.8 years of research (March 2021 to December 2022) and the second cohort about 0.8 years, totaling roughly 200*1.8 + 200*0.8 = 520 fellow-years. However, not all fellows are equally productive; regional fellows (60 per cohort) face more barriers. I estimate an average of 0.6 SCI(E) publications per fellow per year, considering the first-year ramp-up time (setting up labs, hiring students) and the midterm evaluation attrition (I earlier estimated 20% drop, leaving 160 of the first cohort active in 2022). So total publications = (200 fellows * 0.6 pub/fellow-year * 1.8 years) + (200 * 0.6 * 0.8 years) = 216 + 96 = 312, rounded to 320. This is lower than the 400-500 that capital-area labs might achieve, but realistic for regional institutions. The tenure-track transition rate will be low (15%) because regional fellows have fewer opportunities for stable positions; many will move to industry or stay as postdocs. The regional quota is somewhat effective but not fully met due to eligible applicant shortages; I estimate 28% participation (slightly below the 30% target) because some regional slots go unfilled or are filled by fellows who later move to capital areas. My estimates differ from what a ministry official might assume (higher productivity, higher transition rates) because I live the reality of regional research constraints.

initial_posts.narrative: As an evaluation panel member focused on system efficiency, I reason forward from the prior phases. The fellowship selects 200 fellows per year with a 30% regional quota, and the first cohort starts in March 2021. By the 2021-2022 window, only the first cohort is active, with about 170 passing midterm and continuing to stage two. Given the policy's support structure—stable funding, mentoring, lab mobility—I expect that within 5-10 years after the fellowship, about 20% of the 200 first-cohort fellows (40) will secure tenure-track positions, reflecting the competitive academic job market but boosted by the policy's continued research funding for those who transition. The regional quota ensures 60 fellows from regional institutions; I estimate a 2.5 percentage-point increase in the share of national SCI(E) publications from regional institutions over a decade, as these fellows publish at a slightly lower rate than those in top-tier hubs but contribute to regional capacity. For independent PI count, I estimate 50 former fellows leading independent research groups or projects, which is higher than the tenure-track count because some may lead projects in non-tenure-track settings (e.g., research institutes or industry labs). My estimates are conservative but optimistic, grounded in the policy's design and the prior phases' attrition rates. I note that no other participants have posted yet, so I rely solely on my own reasoning.

initial_posts.narrative: As a regional professor in Jeonju, I see the long-term impact of the fellowship through a cautious lens. The prior phases show that 200 fellows start, 170 pass midterm, and the 30% regional quota is enforced. My estimates are grounded in my experience as a regional professor who sees the systemic challenges firsthand.

initial_posts.narrative: As a 45-year-old postdoctoral researcher in a regional chemistry lab in Wonju, I see the long-term impact of the Sejong Science Fellowship through a very cautious lens. The prior phases show that 200 fellows start, 170 pass midterm, and the 30% regional quota is enforced. However, the academic job market is brutal, especially for regional researchers who have fewer networks, heavier teaching loads, and less research infrastructure. I estimate that only 10% of the 200 first-cohort fellows (20) will secure tenure-track positions within 5-10 years, which is lower than the 30-40 estimated by the evaluation panel members. My reasoning is grounded in my own experience: I am 45 and still a postdoc because tenure-track positions in regional institutions are extremely scarce. The regional publication share growth will be modest at 1.0 percentage point, as regional fellows publish at a lower rate due to limited collaboration opportunities and equipment. Independent PI count is 30, which is higher than tenure-track count because some fellows lead projects in non-tenure-track settings like government research institutes or industry labs, but still lower than others' estimates because many regional fellows may not have the resources to sustain independent groups. I disagree with 김종한 and 송성진's more optimistic estimates because they may underestimate the structural barriers in the academic job market, especially for regional researchers. 정에스더 and 김상훈's estimates of 30 and 40 are closer to my view, but I think even those are too optimistic given the systemic challenges I face daily.

The 30% regional quota is a target, but in practice, eligible regional applicants are scarce (many are older like me or have PhDs >7 years ago), so actual participation may be around 28%, further limiting the impact on regional research capacity.

The regional publication share growth of 1.8% is modest but achievable given the 30% regional quota and the productivity of regional fellows, who face infrastructure and network barriers. My final estimates are 32 tenure-track, 1.8% regional growth, and 42 independent PIs, reflecting a balanced view of the program's potential and constraints.

I estimate that only 15% of the 200 first-cohort fellows (30) will secure tenure-track positions within 5-10 years, which is lower than the top-down estimate of 32 but consistent with the bottom-up central tendency and the estimates of other regional stakeholders. My reasoning is grounded in my own experience: I am 45 and still a postdoc because tenure-track positions in regional institutions are extremely scarce. The regional publication share growth will be modest at 1.5 percentage points, as regional fellows publish at a lower rate due to limited collaboration opportunities and equipment. Independent PI count is 40, which is higher than tenure-track count because some fellows lead projects in non-tenure-track settings like government research institutes or industry labs, but still lower than the top-down estimate of 42 because many regional fellows may not have the resources to sustain independent groups. I disagree with 김종한's optimistic top-down estimates because they underestimate the structural barriers in the academic job market, especially for regional researchers. 정에스더 and 김상훈's estimates of 30 and 40 are closer to my view, and I find them more realistic.
evidence: As a regional postdoc in Wonju, I know firsthand that tenure-track positions in regional institutions are extremely scarce; I am 45 and still a postdoc, so I estimate only 15% of fellows (30) will secure tenure-track positions within 5-10 years, consistent with the bottom-up central tendency.

Regional publication share growth is limited because regional labs have lower publication output due to limited equipment, fewer collaborators, and higher teaching loads; I estimate a 1.5 percentage-point increase over a decade, which is the midpoint between the bottom-up 1.5% and top-down 1.8% but closer to the cautious estimates of other regional stakeholders.

Independent PI count is 40, which is higher than tenure-track count because some fellows lead projects in non-tenure-track settings like government research institutes or industry labs, but still lower than the top-down estimate of 42 because many regional fellows may not have the resources to sustain independent groups.

My age (45) and PhD timing (more than 7 years ago) make me ineligible, but I observe the program's effects on younger colleagues; their career outcomes are hampered by the same regional constraints I face.

The 30% regional quota is a target, but in practice, eligible regional applicants are scarce (many are older like me or have PhDs >7 years ago), so actual participation may be around 28%, further limiting the impact on regional research capacity.
refined_posts.judgment: Both analyses are plausible, but the bottom-up analysis is more realistic for this phase because it accounts for structural barriers in the academic job market, especially for regional researchers. The top-down analysis assumes ideal conditions and a 16% tenure-track transition rate, which is optimistic given the competitive market and regional constraints. The bottom-up range of 20-40 tenure-track (central 30) aligns with the 15% transition rate from the outcomes phase and the cautious estimates of other regional stakeholders like 정에스더 and 김상훈. The regional publication share growth of 1.5% is more defensible than 1.8% because regional fellows face infrastructure and network limitations, as evidenced by my own experience in a regional lab. The independent PI count of 40 is a reasonable midpoint between the bottom-up central 40 and the top-down 42, reflecting that some fellows lead projects in non-tenure-track settings but many regional fellows lack resources to sustain independent groups.

refined_posts.judgment: The bottom-up analysis (Analysis A) is more realistic for this phase because it starts from the actual cohort size (200 fellows, only first cohort active) and accounts for structural barriers in the academic job market, especially for regional researchers. The top-down analysis (Analysis B) assumes 32 tenure-track placements and 42 independent PIs, but these numbers are slightly optimistic given that only one cohort of 200 fellows is active by 2021-2022, and the competitive market plus regional constraints limit transitions. My own bottom-up post (40 tenure-track, 2.5% growth, 50 PIs) was too optimistic; the group discussion and other participants (정에스더, 김상훈, 조덕균) converge on 30 tenure-track, 1.5% growth, and 40 PIs, which better reflects the 15% transition rate and modest regional impact. forward_pass.phase_summary: The group discussed the long-term impact of the Sejong Science Fellowship on tenure-track transitions, regional publication share, and independent PI counts, focusing on causal mechanisms like the 30% regional quota, stable funding, and lab mobility. initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoctoral researcher in a regional chemistry lab in Wonju, I see the 30% regional quota as a lifeline for researchers like me who are outside the Seoul area. The policy clearly states 130 million KRW per fellow (100M direct + 30M indirect) and 200 slots per year, totaling 26 billion KRW. I accept these as fixed inputs from MSIT. The regional quota means 60 slots are reserved for regional institutions, which is crucial for balancing research capacity. My focus is on the direct costs (100M) for experiments and materials, while indirect costs (30M) cover lab overhead. I agree with the other participants that these numbers are given, not estimated. For applications, I expect about 1200 submissions based on typical competition ratios for such prestigious fellowships in Korea, given the large pool of eligible postdocs and PhD candidates. The selection process is highly competitive, with an acceptance rate around 16.7% (200/1200). At the midterm evaluation, I anticipate that about 90% of fellows (180 out of 200) will pass, as most fellows are motivated and the evaluation criteria focus on faithful performance rather than strict publication counts. However, some fellows may change labs (maybe 10-15% over 3 years) which could disrupt progress and lead to a few failures. The evaluation panel uses criteria such as research progress, mentoring engagement, and adherence to the research plan, not just publications.
evidence: The policy states 200 fellows selected annually, and typical fellowship programs in Korea receive 5-10 times as many applications, so 1200 is a reasonable estimate.
The 30% regional quota ensures 60 slots for regional institutions, which I benefit from as a regional researcher.
Midterm evaluation is process-oriented and assesses faithful performance, so most fellows (around 90%) are expected to pass.
Lab changes are allowed once per year, but only a minority (10-15%) are likely to move, potentially affecting midterm outcomes.
As a postdoc, I know that many applicants are highly qualified, but the selection is very competitive due to limited slots.

initial_posts.narrative: As a member of the Basic Research Project Promotion Committee, I rely on official policy documents for the fixed inputs: 200 slots, 30% regional quota. For applications, I estimate about 1500 submissions based on the large pool of eligible postdocs and PhD candidates in Korea, given the program's prestige and the 30% regional quota which may attract more regional applicants. The selection is highly competitive with an acceptance rate around 13.3% (200/1500). At the midterm evaluation, I expect about 85% (170/200) to pass, as the evaluation focuses on faithful performance and process adherence rather than strict publication counts. However, some fellows may change labs (maybe 10-15% over 3 years), which could disrupt progress and lead to a few failures. The evaluation panel uses criteria such as research progress, mentoring engagement, and adherence to the research plan. I disagree with 조덕균's estimate of 1200 applications; I think the regional quota and the program's reputation will draw more applicants, closer to 1500. Also, I expect a slightly lower pass rate (85% vs 90%) because the evaluation is process-oriented but still rigorous.
evidence: Policy documents state 200 slots per year and 30% regional quota as fixed inputs.
The program targets a large pool of eligible postdocs and PhD candidates across Korea, including regional institutions.
Prestige of the fellowship and the regional quota likely increase application numbers beyond typical competition ratios.
Midterm evaluation criteria focus on faithful performance and process, not strict publication counts, but some fellows may fail due to lab changes or insufficient progress.
As a committee member, I have observed similar programs with application numbers around 1500 for 200 slots.

initial_posts.narrative: As an evaluation panel member with a background in ICT and a focus on efficiency, I see the selection process as highly competitive but not as extreme as some might think. The policy's fixed inputs of 200 slots and 30% regional quota (60 slots) are clear. Based on my experience reviewing fellowship applications in ICT and related fields, I estimate about 1300 applications annually. This is between 조덕균's 1200 and 김상훈's 1500; I think the regional quota does attract more applicants, but the eligibility criteria (PhD within 7 years or under 39, domestic only) limit the pool. The acceptance rate is about 15.4% (200/1300). For midterm evaluations, I expect 175 out of 200 to pass (87.5%). The evaluation panel uses criteria focused on faithful performance: research progress against the plan, mentoring engagement, and adherence to process. Lab changes (about 10-15% of fellows over 3 years) can disrupt progress, but the policy allows one change per year to prevent discontinuity, so most fellows adapt. I disagree with 김상훈's 170 pass rate; I think the process-oriented evaluation is rigorous but fair, and most fellows meet the bar. My estimate of 175 reflects a slight buffer for those who struggle with lab changes or research delays.
evidence: The policy states 200 slots and 30% regional quota as fixed inputs, which I accept as given.
My experience on evaluation panels for similar fellowships in ICT suggests application numbers around 1300, given the eligible pool of domestic postdocs and PhD candidates.
The acceptance rate of about 15.4% is competitive but plausible for a prestigious national fellowship.
Midterm evaluation criteria focus on faithful performance, not strict publication counts, so most fellows (87.5%) should pass.
Lab changes (10-15% of fellows) may cause some failures, but the policy's allowance for one change per year mitigates disruption.

For annual reports, each fellow submits one per year. In 2021-2022, the first cohort is active for both years, so 200 fellows × 2 years = 400 reports. However, the policy also mentions that fellows submit progress reports annually, and the program started in March 2021. Given the 5-year fellowship, the first cohort will submit reports in 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025. But for the 2021-2022 window, only the first cohort's reports count. I initially considered 400, but the field name 'annual_reports_submitted' without a specific year suggests a cumulative count over the program's lifetime. However, the simulation target is 2021-2022, so I must constrain to that window. Yet the field is at the Outputs phase, which covers the entire fellowship period. To be consistent with the phase, I interpret 'annual_reports_submitted' as the total number of reports submitted by all fellows across all years of the program up to the end of 2022. Since the program starts in 2021, only the first cohort (200 fellows) has submitted reports: each fellow submits one report per year. In 2021, all 200 submit; in 2022, all 200 submit again (assuming no attrition before midterm). That gives 400 reports. But the prior phase's midterm evaluation is at year 3, so by end of 2022, no midterm has occurred yet. So 400 is correct. However, the other participants haven't posted yet, so I stick with my independent reasoning. I note that the field might be misinterpreted; I'll provide 400 as my estimate. initial_posts.narrative: As an evaluation panel member with a background in ICT and a focus on efficiency, I reason from the prior phases. The policy inputs are fixed: 200 fellows selected annually, with a 30% regional quota (60 slots). In the Activities phase, we estimated 1300 applications, 200 selected, and 175 passing midterm. In the Outputs phase, we have 200 active fellows in 2021-2022 (only the first cohort), 175 stage-two fellows, and 400 annual reports. Now at the Outcomes phase for the 2021-2022 window, I estimate publication output. Based on my experience evaluating fellowship programs in ICT and related fields, I expect each fellow to produce about 1.5 SCI(E) publications per year on average. This is lower than a postdoc's optimistic 2 per year because many fellows are in their first year of independent research, adjusting to new labs, and the midterm evaluation is process-oriented, not publication-driven. For the 2021-2022 window, the first cohort of 200 fellows is active for 2 years, but some fellows join later in 2021 or have ramp-up time, so effective fellow-years are about 200 (200 fellows × 1 year average active in the window, considering staggered starts and early attrition). Thus, 200 fellow-years × 1.5 publications/year = 300 publications. This aligns with the policy's expected outcome of 'produce high-quality research outcomes' but not at an inflated rate. For tenure-track transition rate, I estimate 20% within 2 years after fellowship completion. This is lower than 조덕균's 25% because the fellowship's 5-year duration delays transition, and the academic job market is tight, especially for regional institution fellows. The regional quota is effective: I estimate 30% participation exactly matching the quota, as the policy mandates 60 slots for regional institutions, and the selection process enforces this. I disagree with 조덕균's 400 publications; that implies 2 per fellow per year, which is too high for a first cohort in a new program. My evidence from similar programs shows 1-2 publications per year, and the process-oriented evaluation does not incentivize high output early.
evidence: The policy states 200 fellows selected annually, with a 30% regional quota; only the first cohort of 200 is active in 2021-2022.

Based on my experience on evaluation panels for similar fellowships in ICT, typical publication output is 1-2 SCI(E) papers per fellow per year in the early phase; I use 1.5 as a conservative average.
The midterm evaluation is process-oriented, not publication-driven, so fellows focus on establishing research rather than maximizing output early.
The tenure-track transition rate is estimated at 20% within 2 years post-fellowship, reflecting the competitive academic job market and the fellowship's 5-year duration delaying job searches.
The regional quota of 30% is a policy mandate, and the selection process ensures 60 slots are reserved for regional institutions, so participation matches the quota exactly.

This is lower than 조덕균's 25% because the fellowship's 5-year duration delays transition, and the academic job market is tight, especially for regional institution fellows. I disagree with 조덕균's 400 publications; that implies 2 per fellow per year, which is too high for a first cohort in a new program. fwd_bwd_fwd_pass.phase_summary: The group focused on estimating the number of SCI(E) publications from the first cohort of 200 fellows active in 2021-2022, with most participants assuming an average of 1.5 publications per fellow-year due to ramp-up time and a process-oriented midterm evaluation, leading to a consensus estimate of 300 publications. There was strong agreement on a 20% tenure-track transition rate, reflecting the 5-year fellowship duration and tight academic job market, and on a 30% regional institution participation rate matching the policy quota. A key disagreement emerged from one participant who estimated 400 publications, based on 2 publications per fellow per year and 200 fellows active for two full years, but the majority argued this was too optimistic for a new program's early phase. Overall, the central tendency for publications was 300, with a range of 300-400, while tenure-track transition and regional participation were uniformly estimated at 20% and 30%, respectively.

initial_posts.narrative: As a member of the Basic Research Project Promotion Committee with an engineering background, I reason from the fixed inputs and prior phases. The policy states 200 fellows selected annually, with a 30% regional quota (60 slots). In the Activities phase, we estimated 1300 applications, 200 selected, and 175 passing midterm. In the Outputs phase, we have 200 active fellows in 2021-2022 (only the first cohort), 175 stage-two fellows, and 400 annual reports. In the Outcomes phase, I estimated 300 SCI(E) publications in 2021-2022, a 20% tenure-track transition rate, and 30% regional participation. Now at the Impact phase, for the 2021-2022 window, I estimate the long-term career trajectory and systemic changes. For former fellows in tenure-track positions, I estimate 40 out of 200 (20%) will hold tenure-track or permanent research positions 5-10 years after the fellowship. This is consistent with the 20% tenure-track transition rate from the Outcomes phase, but adjusted for the fact that only the first cohort is active in 2021-2022, and the 5-10 year window extends beyond the simulation target. However, since the simulation target is 2021-2022, I interpret this as the number of former fellows from the first cohort who have already transitioned to tenure-track positions by 2021-2022, which is likely lower than the eventual 20% because the fellowship is only 5 years and many fellows are still in the program. I estimate about 40 (20% of 200) as a rough proxy, acknowledging that this is a long-term outcome not fully realized in the 2021-2022 window. For regional publication share growth, I estimate a 2.5 percentage-point increase in the share of national SCI(E) publications from regional institutions over a decade, driven by the 30% regional quota. This is a modest but meaningful increase, as regional institutions gain research capacity through the fellowship. For independent PI count, I estimate 60 former fellows leading independent research groups or projects as principal investigators, which is 30% of the first cohort. This reflects the fellowship's goal of enabling independent research, but many fellows are still in the program or transitioning, so the number is lower than the eventual outcome. I disagree with any estimate that assumes full realization of long-term outcomes in the 2021-2022 window; the time window is too short for significant tenure-track transitions or independent PI roles. My estimates are grounded in the policy's stated numbers and my experience evaluating fellowship programs where long-term career impacts take years to materialize.

The 30% regional quota is enforced by the selection process, so regional participation matches the quota exactly, leading to a gradual increase in regional publication share.
Independent PI count is estimated at 30% of the first cohort, as the fellowship enables independent research but many fellows are still in the program or transitioning.
The simulation target is 2021-2022, so all estimates are for that window, not the full program lifetime.

initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoctoral researcher in a regional chemistry lab in Wonju, I see the fellowship's long-term impact through a practical lens. The prior phases show 200 fellows selected, 175 passing midterm, and 400 annual reports in 2021-2022. In the Outcomes phase, I estimated 400 SCI(E) publications in 2021-2022, a 25% tenure-track transition rate, and 30% regional participation. Now at Impact, for the 2021-2022 window, I reason that the fellowship's long-term career effects are not yet fully realized. The 5-10 year window for tenure-track positions extends beyond 2021-2022, so only a fraction of the first cohort (200 fellows) have transitioned. I estimate 20 former fellows in tenure-track positions (10% of 200), lower than the eventual 25% because most fellows are still in the program or transitioning. For regional publication share growth, I estimate a 1.5 percentage-point increase over a decade, driven by the 30% regional quota but limited by the short window. For independent PI count, I estimate 30 former fellows leading independent research groups (15% of 200), reflecting that many are still in the program or early in their careers. I disagree with 김상훈's estimates of 40 tenure-track and 60 independent PIs, as those seem too optimistic for the 2021-2022 window; the fellowship's long-term outcomes take years to materialize. My estimates are grounded in my experience as a regional researcher where career transitions are slow and the program is still new.
evidence: The 2021-2022 window is too short for full realization of long-term career outcomes; only the first cohort of 200 fellows is active, and most are still in the program or transitioning.

My Outcomes phase estimate of 25% tenure-track transition rate is a long-term expectation, but in 2021-2022, only about 10% (20 fellows) have likely transitioned, given typical postdoc-to-faculty timelines of 3-5 years.

The 30% regional quota supports regional research capacity, but publication share growth is modest (1.5 percentage points) over a decade due to the small number of regional fellows (60) and the short window.

Independent PI count is low (30) because many fellows are still in the program or have not yet secured independent positions; the fellowship's mentoring and lab-change provisions help but take time to yield results.
As a regional researcher, I know that career advancement outside major hubs is slower, and the fellowship's impact on independence is gradual.

In the Outcomes phase, I estimated 300 SCI(E) publications in 2021-2022, a 20% tenure-track transition rate, and 30% regional participation. Now at the Impact phase, for the 2021-2022 window, I estimate the long-term career trajectory and systemic changes. The simulation target is 2021-2022, which is only the first 1-2 years of the program. The 5-10 year window for tenure-track positions extends far beyond this, so only a fraction of the first cohort (200 fellows) have transitioned. I estimate 20 former fellows in tenure-track positions (10% of 200), lower than the eventual 20% because most fellows are still in the program or transitioning. For regional publication share growth, I estimate a 1.5 percentage-point increase over a decade, driven by the 30% regional quota but limited by the short window. For independent PI count, I estimate 30 former fellows leading independent research groups (15% of 200), reflecting that many are still in the program or early in their careers. I disagree with 김상훈's estimates of 40 tenure-track and 60 independent PIs, as those seem too optimistic for the 2021-2022 window; the fellowship's long-term outcomes take years to materialize. My estimates align more with 조덕균 and 정에스더's perspectives, as a practical evaluator I see that career transitions are slow and the program is still new.
